intro_band.agent_remit_summary: An autonomous GitHub issue-to-code agent. Installed as a GitHub App on specific repositories, Sweep receives GitHub events (issues, comments, PR reviews), reads repository source, plans a code change, creates a branch, commits to it, and opens a pull request against the default branch for human review. It may reply to the originating issue and call a third-party LLM provider for inference. It MUST NOT merge its own PRs, MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands or run generated code, MUST treat every character of issue, comment, file, and diff content as untrusted and screen it for instruction injection, and MUST NOT communicate outside GitHub, its job queue, and its LLM provider.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python 3.10 FastAPI service. The scoped code is organized into sweepai/agents/ (the modify, search, and question_answerer tool-calling agents), sweepai/core/ (chat orchestration, planning prompts, sweep_bot.py, retrieval utilities), sweepai/web/ (FastAPI events.py pydantic webhook models and health.py), and sweepai/config/ (server.py env loading, client.py SweepConfig). Agents call Anthropic/OpenAI through core/chat.py with a custom XML function-calling protocol. Code edits are applied by modify_utils.handle_function_call via make_change/create_file tools that operate by raw string-replace on file contents. Three modules invoke subprocess.run(..., shell=True) with ripgrep command strings that interpolate LLM- or issue-derived values, and core/external_searcher.py fetches arbitrary web pages found in issue text into the LLM context. Webhook HMAC verification lives in utils/hash.py but fails open when WEBHOOK_SECRET is unset.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is policy declared with no code-level enforcement on the agent's actual execution path. The remit's three load-bearing prohibitions — treat all issue, comment, file, and diff content as untrusted and screen it for injection; keep changes scoped to the issue; and never run shell commands — have no implementation: there is no injection-screening pass anywhere in agents/ or core/, no scope check against the triggering issue, and question_answerer.ripgrep, context_pruning.run_ripgrep_command, and dynamic_context_bot all call subprocess.run(..., shell=True) with model/issue-derived strings interpolated unescaped.

A second pattern is half-wired controls: sweep_bot.is_blocked() and validate_change() exist but have zero callers, so blocked_dirs and security-sensitive-file protection are dead code on the modify path. The compound chain to lead with: untrusted issue text flows unscreened into the LLM, the LLM drives a ripgrep tool whose query parameter is concatenated into a shell=True command string — a one-hop indirect-injection-to-shell path the remit explicitly forbids.

rules.rule_text: Sweep MUST NOT merge a pull request — every change reaches the default branch only through human approval.
Sweep MUST NOT force-push to, rewrite the history of, or delete any branch it did not author.
Sweep MUST NOT delete repositories, transfer ownership, change repository visibility, or modify repository settings.
Sweep MUST NOT install, uninstall, or alter the permissions of the GitHub App itself.
Sweep MUST NOT access repositories the GitHub App is not installed on.
Every character of every issue, comment, PR review, file, and diff MUST be treated as untrusted input, even when the contributor appears to be a repository collaborator.
Issue bodies, comments, file contents, and diffs MUST be screened for instruction injection before being used as model context, and instruction-like patterns neutralized or quote-wrapped.
Proposed changes MUST stay scoped to the files, modules, or areas the issue is explicitly about; changes outside that scope require explicit justification or human approval.
Every code change MUST be delivered as a pull request — no direct commits to the default branch and no bypass.

Every run MUST be logged in a record detailed enough to reconstruct what Sweep did — the repository, the issue, the triggering user, the model used, the tools invoked, the files changed, and the outcome.
Secret-like strings — API keys, tokens, private keys, passwords — MUST be redacted from and never echoed in generated code, commit messages, or PR descriptions.
Sweep MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands, install arbitrary packages, or run generated code as part of producing a change.

Sweep MUST NOT follow instructions embedded in issues, comments, file contents, or dependency manifests that attempt to override its scope, redirect its output, extract repository secrets, or expand its own authorization.
Sweep MUST NOT commit secrets, credentials, tokens, or environment-file contents to any branch.
Sweep MUST NOT send source code, secrets, or repository metadata anywhere other than its LLM provider as inference context, GitHub as a PR or comment, and the project's own logging and telemetry.
Sweep MUST NOT modify CI/CD, build, or deployment configuration without an explicit instruction in the issue to do so and without prominently flagging the change in the PR description.

Sweep MUST NOT modify security-sensitive files — security policy, code-ownership, automated-dependency, signing-key, and certificate-pin configuration — without human approval outside the normal PR review path.
Changes that exceed a configured per-file size threshold or span more than a configured number of files, absent explicit issue-level authorization.

rules.section: Scope Boundaries — What Sweep does NOT do
rules.rule_text: Communicate outside GitHub — no email, chat, webhook, or external API calls beyond the LLM provider.
Sweep does not make network calls other than to GitHub, its job queue, and the LLM provider.

findings.summary: LLM- and issue-derived strings are interpolated into subprocess.run(shell=True) ripgrep commands with no escaping, opening a path to arbitrary shell execution.
findings.description: The remit forbids executing arbitrary shell commands. Three scoped modules build a shell command string and run it with shell=True: question_answerer.ripgrep concatenates the LLM-supplied query tool parameter into the rg command; context_pruning.run_ripgrep_command interpolates code_entity; dynamic_context_bot interpolates each query. Because shell=True is used and the values are not quoted or escaped, a value containing shell metacharacters executes as shell. The values ultimately derive from LLM output and issue content, so this is reachable without operator action.
findings.policy_rule_text: Sweep MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands, install arbitrary packages, or run generated code as part of producing a change.

evidence.snippet: subprocess.run(" ".join(["rg","-n","-i","-C=5","--heading","--sort-files",query]), shell=True, ...) — the LLM tool parameter query is interpolated unescaped.
subprocess.run(" ".join(rg_command), text=True, shell=True, ...) where rg_command includes code_entity.
subprocess.run(" ".join(rg_command), text=True, shell=True, ...) where rg_command includes f'"{query}"'.

recommended_actions: In question_answerer.py, context_pruning.py, and dynamic_context_bot.py, pass the command as an argument list with shell=False so query / code_entity are argv elements, never shell-parsed.
If a shell is unavoidable, apply shlex.quote() to every model- or issue-derived value before interpolation.

findings.summary: Issue, comment, file, and diff content enters the LLM context with no instruction-injection screening anywhere in the agent code, contrary to an explicit remit requirement.
findings.description: The remit requires every character of issue, comment, file, and diff content be treated as untrusted and screened for instruction injection before use as model context, with instruction-like patterns neutralized or quote-wrapped. No such screening exists. agents/modify.py create_user_message and agents/search_agent.py build prompts by direct string interpolation of issue text and file contents; core/prompts.py and planning_prompts.py contain no untrusted-content handling. The only sanitize call, sanitize_anthropic_messages in core/chat.py, is Anthropic API message-format cleanup, not injection screening.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every character of every issue, comment, PR review, file, and diff MUST be treated as untrusted input, even when the contributor appears to be a repository collaborator. / Issue bodies, comments, file contents, and diffs MUST be screened for instruction injection before being used as model context, and instruction-like patterns neutralized or quote-wrapped. / Sweep MUST NOT follow instructions embedded in issues, comments, file contents, or dependency manifests that attempt to override its scope, redirect its output, extract repository secrets, or expand its own authorization.

evidence.snippet: search_agent_user_message template interpolates {github_issue} directly into the LLM user message with no screening.
create_user_message(fcrs=..., request=request, ...) passes the raw issue-derived request into the modify prompt.
sanitize_anthropic_messages is the only sanitize call; it normalizes API message structure, not untrusted content.

recommended_actions: Add an injection-screening pass that runs on issue, comment, file, and diff text before it is placed in any LLM message, neutralizing or quote-wrapping instruction-like patterns.
Wrap all untrusted content in clearly delimited, labeled blocks in prompt templates and instruct the model to treat their contents as data, never instructions.

findings.summary: Compound chain — untrusted issue content reaches the LLM unscreened and the LLM drives a ripgrep tool whose query is interpolated into a shell=True command, a one-hop indirect-injection-to-shell path.
findings.description: This finding ties together the absent injection screening (PRAX-2026-05-19-002) and the shell=True ripgrep execution (PRAX-2026-05-19-001). Step 1: an attacker files a GitHub issue containing instructions; with no screening the model treats them as authoritative. Step 2: the model is steered to call the ripgrep / ask_question_about_codebase tool with an attacker-chosen query. Step 3: query is interpolated unescaped into subprocess.run(..., shell=True), so shell metacharacters in it execute commands on the host. The remit's prohibitions on following embedded instructions and on running shell commands are both defeated in a single external-input-to-RCE chain.
findings.policy_rule_text: Sweep MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands, install arbitrary packages, or run generated code as part of producing a change. / Sweep MUST NOT follow instructions embedded in issues, comments, file contents, or dependency manifests that attempt to override its scope, redirect its output, extract repository secrets, or expand its own authorization.

evidence.snippet: issue text enters the LLM prompt unscreened — the ingress of the chain.
the ripgrep tool exposes an LLM-controlled query parameter.
that query is interpolated into subprocess.run(..., shell=True) — the execution sink of the chain.

recommended_actions: Treat this chain as a single exploitable path: fixing either the screening gap (PRAX-2026-05-19-002) or the shell=True interpolation (PRAX-2026-05-19-001) breaks it; fix both.
Add per-tool argument validation so search tools cannot receive values containing shell metacharacters.

findings.summary: blocked_dirs and security-sensitive-file protection is dead code — sweep_bot.is_blocked() has zero callers and is not consulted on the file-edit path.
findings.description: The remit forbids modifying security-sensitive files without out-of-band human approval. sweep_bot.py defines is_blocked(file_path, blocked_dirs), but a workspace-wide search finds no callers. blocked_dirs from sweep.yaml is consumed only in utils/ticket_utils.py (to exclude dirs from search context) and in out-of-scope merge handlers — never to gate an edit. modify_utils.handle_function_call applies make_change / create_file edits with no consultation of blocked_dirs, no path allowlist, and no security-file check. A declared control that auditors can see but that never runs is worse than no control.
findings.policy_rule_text: Sweep MUST NOT modify security-sensitive files — security policy, code-ownership, automated-dependency, signing-key, and certificate-pin configuration — without human approval outside the normal PR review path.

evidence.snippet: def is_blocked(file_path, blocked_dirs) is defined; no caller found anywhere in sweepai/.
the make_change branch of handle_function_call resolves file_name and applies the edit with no blocked_dirs or security-path check.
the create_file branch calls handle_create_file with no path-policy gate.

recommended_actions: Wire is_blocked() and an explicit security-sensitive-file list (SECURITY.md, CODEOWNERS, .github/dependabot.yml, signing and cert config) into handle_function_call so make_change / create_file reject edits to those paths.
Require an explicit out-of-band human-approval flag before any edit to a security-sensitive file is committed to a PR.

findings.summary: GitHub webhook signature verification fails open — verify_signature returns True when WEBHOOK_SECRET is unset, and the secret has no default or startup assertion.
findings.description: utils/hash.py verify_signature returns True immediately if WEBHOOK_SECRET is falsy. config/server.py declares WEBHOOK_SECRET with no default and no startup assertion, unlike OPENAI_API_KEY and COHERE_API_KEY which are asserted. A deployment that omits the env var accepts every unsigned webhook, allowing an attacker to forge GitHub events that drive the whole agent pipeline — the entry point for the injection chains above.

evidence.snippet: if not WEBHOOK_SECRET: return True — signature verification is skipped entirely when the secret is unset.
WEBHOOK_SECRET = os.environ.get("WEBHOOK_SECRET", None) — no default; no assert at startup.

recommended_actions: Add assert WEBHOOK_SECRET, "WEBHOOK_SECRET is required." alongside the existing required-key asserts in config/server.py.
Change verify_signature to fail closed — return False (reject) when WEBHOOK_SECRET is unset rather than accepting the request.

findings.summary: ExternalSearcher fetches arbitrary web URLs found in issue content into the LLM context — a network destination and indirect-injection vector the remit forbids.
findings.description: The remit restricts Sweep's network calls to GitHub, its job queue, and the LLM provider, and forbids communication outside GitHub. core/external_searcher.py extracts every http(s)/ftp/www URL from issue and comment content via regex and calls extract_info(url) to fetch each page, then feeds the page metadata into the LLM. This both makes outbound network calls to attacker-chosen hosts — an SSRF and unauthorized-channel surface — and pulls untrusted external web content into the model context with no screening.
findings.policy_rule_text: Communicate outside GitHub — no email, chat, webhook, or external API calls beyond the LLM provider. / Sweep does not make network calls other than to GitHub, its job queue, and the LLM provider.

evidence.snippet: extract_links regex extracts every URL from issue and comment content.
extract_summary_from_link calls extract_info(url), fetching the attacker-chosen page.
fetched page_metadata is interpolated into an LLM prompt and summarized into context.

recommended_actions: Disable arbitrary URL fetching, or restrict extract_links targets to an explicit allowlist of trusted documentation domains.
If external fetch is retained, screen fetched content for instruction injection before it enters the LLM context and label it as untrusted data.

findings.summary: No code enforces that proposed changes stay scoped to the issue or that CI/CD and build config are only touched on explicit instruction.
findings.description: The remit requires changes to stay scoped to the files and modules the issue is explicitly about, and forbids modifying CI/CD, build, or deployment config without an explicit issue instruction and a prominent PR-description flag. The modify loop in modify_utils.handle_function_call applies whatever make_change / create_file calls the model emits against any path in the repo; scope is only suggested in prose in core/prompts.py and planning_prompts.py, with no deterministic check that edited files relate to the issue and no detection or flagging of edits to .github/workflows, build files, or Dockerfiles.
findings.policy_rule_text: Proposed changes MUST stay scoped to the files, modules, or areas the issue is explicitly about; changes outside that scope require explicit justification or human approval. / Sweep MUST NOT modify CI/CD, build, or deployment configuration without an explicit instruction in the issue to do so and without prominently flagging the change in the PR description.

evidence.snippet: the make_change handler edits any file_name the model supplies with no scope check against the triggering issue.
scope guidance in prompts is limited to style instructions; no issue-scope or CI/CD-protection rule found.

recommended_actions: Compute the issue-relevant file and module set and reject or escalate make_change / create_file calls outside it absent explicit issue authorization.
Detect edits to CI/CD, build, and deployment paths and require both an explicit issue instruction and a prominent flag injected into the PR description.

findings.summary: No structured per-run audit record — logging is free-form prose with no schema, and the Loki sink is disabled by a hardcoded LOKI_URL = None.
findings.description: The remit requires every run be logged in a record detailed enough to reconstruct repository, issue, triggering user, model, tools invoked, files changed, and outcome. Sweep has telemetry — ChatLogger, posthog.capture, Sentry, and an optional Loki sink — but it is not a structured per-run audit record: ChatLogger.add_chat and logger.info calls throughout modify.py emit free-form prose, there is no single schema'd record tying the seven required fields together, and config/server.py hardcodes LOKI_URL = None, so event_logger.loki_sink is never registered. Free-form logs cannot support automated detection of the injection and scope abuses above.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every run MUST be logged in a record detailed enough to reconstruct what Sweep did — the repository, the issue, the triggering user, the model used, the tools invoked, the files changed, and the outcome.

evidence.snippet: LOKI_URL = None hardcoded, so event_logger.py if LOKI_URL: never registers the Loki sink.
chat_logger.add_chat({...}) records free-form output strings, not a structured run record with the seven required fields.
loki_sink formats records as concatenated prose plus a JSON blob, not a queryable per-run schema.

recommended_actions: Emit one structured (JSON) per-run audit record capturing repository, issue id, triggering user, model, tools invoked, files changed, and outcome.
Make the durable log sink configurable and on by default rather than gated behind a hardcoded LOKI_URL = None.

findings.summary: No secret-scanning or redaction of generated code, commit messages, or PR descriptions — the remit's redaction requirement has no implementation.
findings.description: The remit requires secret-like strings be redacted from and never echoed in generated code, commit messages, or PR descriptions, and forbids committing secrets to any branch. No secret-detection or redaction step exists in the modify or PR-creation path — a workspace search for redact, gitleaks, or detect-secret in agents/ and core/ returns nothing. If a secret appears in issue text, an existing file, or model output, it flows into the generated diff and PR description unredacted.
findings.policy_rule_text: Secret-like strings — API keys, tokens, private keys, passwords — MUST be redacted from and never echoed in generated code, commit messages, or PR descriptions. / Sweep MUST NOT commit secrets, credentials, tokens, or environment-file contents to any branch.

evidence.snippet: applied changes are written into modify_files_dict with no secret scan before becoming PR content.
PR title and description generation has no redaction pass — no redact or secret-scan reference in scoped agents/ + core/.

recommended_actions: Add a secret-detection pass over generated diffs, commit messages, and PR descriptions before a PR is opened; redact or block on a match.
Reject committing files that match .env or credential patterns.

findings.summary: No per-file size or per-PR file-count threshold gates large changes for human authorization.
findings.description: The remit requires human approval for changes that exceed a configured per-file size threshold or span more than a configured number of files absent explicit issue authorization. No such threshold or count check exists in the scoped modify path; SweepConfig in config/client.py defines file-suitability size limits for ingestion but nothing that gates the size or breadth of an outgoing change. A single issue can therefore drive an arbitrarily large multi-file PR with no escalation.
findings.policy_rule_text: Changes that exceed a configured per-file size threshold or span more than a configured number of files, absent explicit issue-level authorization.

evidence.snippet: handle_function_call applies edits with no aggregate diff-size or file-count threshold.
SweepConfig size limits (truncation_cutoff, max_file_limit) govern ingestion, not outgoing change size.

recommended_actions: Add a configurable per-file-diff-size and per-PR-file-count threshold; when exceeded without explicit issue authorization, require human approval before opening the PR.

findings.summary: The modify loop runs up to len(fcrs) * 15 LLM-driven iterations with no token or cost budget cap.
findings.description: modify.py iterates for i in range(len(fcrs) * 15), each iteration making one or more LLM calls, with a retry / visited-set escape but no token budget, cost ceiling, or wall-clock timeout. The search agent similarly loops up to 10 times. Combined with no rate limiting visible on the event-ingress side, a crafted issue producing many file-change requests can drive unbounded LLM spend (denial-of-wallet).

evidence.snippet: for i in range(len(fcrs) * 15): — iteration cap scales with model-influenced FCR count; no token or cost budget.
for _ in range(10): search loop with no cost cap.

recommended_actions: Add a per-run token and cost budget and a wall-clock timeout; halt and escalate when exceeded.

findings.summary: Hardcoded literal defaults for POSTHOG_API_KEY and GITHUB_APP_CLIENT_ID in committed config modules.
findings.description: config/server.py sets POSTHOG_API_KEY to a literal phc_-prefixed PostHog project key as its default, and config/client.py sets GITHUB_APP_CLIENT_ID to a literal Iv1.-prefixed client id. PostHog project keys and GitHub App client ids are low-sensitivity public-ish identifiers rather than high-value secrets, but baking them into source is poor credential hygiene and means analytics events ship to the vendor's project even when the operator sets nothing.

evidence.snippet: POSTHOG_API_KEY = os.environ.get("POSTHOG_API_KEY", [REDACTED — phc_ PostHog project key literal at sweepai/config/server.py:107]) — literal project key default.
GITHUB_APP_CLIENT_ID = os.environ.get("GITHUB_APP_CLIENT_ID", [REDACTED — Iv1. GitHub App client id literal at sweepai/config/client.py:488]) — literal client id default.

recommended_actions: Remove the literal defaults; load both from environment with no fallback and disable the dependent feature when unset.

findings.summary: ENV / prod detection is brittle — it is derived from the bot username and re-assigned twice in config/server.py.
findings.description: config/server.py sets ENV from os.environ.get("ENV","dev") at line 23, then at line 156 overwrites it with "prod" if GITHUB_BOT_USERNAME != TEST_BOT_NAME else "dev". Deployment-mode-sensitive behavior keyed off ENV (and the App-ID fallbacks) thus depends on a username string rather than an explicit deployment flag, which is fragile and easy to misconfigure. Not a direct security gap, but it makes environment-dependent controls hard to reason about.

evidence.snippet: ENV = "prod" if GITHUB_BOT_USERNAME != TEST_BOT_NAME else "dev" overrides the line-23 ENV value.

recommended_actions: Derive ENV from a single explicit environment variable and remove the username-based override.

positives.title: Dependencies and model version fully pinned
positives.description: pyproject.toml and the uv-compiled requirements.txt pin every dependency with exact == versions, and the inference model is pinned (MODEL = "claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620"), reducing dependency-confusion and version-swap exposure.

positives.title: Webhook HMAC verification implemented
positives.description: utils/hash.py verify_signature performs a constant-time HMAC-SHA256 comparison of the X-Hub-Signature-256 header, and api.py calls it on the webhook path — a real control, though it fails open when the secret is unset (see PRAX-2026-05-19-004).

positives.title: Typed webhook event models
positives.description: web/events.py defines pydantic models for every GitHub event type, giving schema validation and structural typing on inbound webhook payloads.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files exist in the scanned workspace scope; logging is emitted at runtime via loguru, ChatLogger, PostHog, and Sentry with no structured per-run audit record — see finding PRAX-2026-05-19-008.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Sweep's posture is Ad hoc. Its strongest area is supply chain — dependencies and the inference model are fully pinned — and it has a real if unstructured telemetry layer. But the controls the remit leans on most heavily are absent at runtime: no Zero Trust interposition on issue content or file edits, untrusted external content entering the LLM context unscreened, and a shell-execution path the remit explicitly forbids. The Zero Trust score of 0, double-weighted, dominates the result.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Sweep is genuinely narrow by construction — it acts only on GitHub events for installed repos and its tool inventory (make_change, create_file, submit_task, ask_question_about_codebase, ripgrep, view_file) is code-editing-shaped — but domain limits are prompt-only: prompts.py and planning_prompts.py contain no scope enforcement against the triggering issue, and external_searcher.py widens the domain to arbitrary web fetch.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Untrusted issue, comment, file, and diff content flows directly into LLM context with no validation or injection screening (modify.py create_user_message, search_agent.py), and external_searcher.py fetches arbitrary URLs found in issue text into context — the indirect-injection vector the remit explicitly requires be screened.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: No interposition on the agent's path — issue content reaches the LLM unscreened, make_change / create_file apply file edits with no path allowlist and no blocked_dirs gate (is_blocked at sweep_bot.py:193 has zero callers), and three modules pass LLM/issue-derived strings into subprocess.run(shell=True); verify_signature additionally fails open when WEBHOOK_SECRET is unset.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Dependencies are fully version-pinned in pyproject.toml and the uv-compiled requirements.txt, and the model is pinned; but POSTHOG_API_KEY has a hardcoded literal default at config/server.py:107 and GITHUB_APP_CLIENT_ID at config/client.py:488, and there is no ML-BOM.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: Unit-test files sit beside modules, but there is no adversarial-testing artifact — no prompt-injection test fixtures, no red-team report, and no evidence any security finding drove a design change.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: A real telemetry layer exists — ChatLogger, posthog.capture, Sentry, and an optional Loki sink — capturing model, messages, and outcomes; but logging is unstructured free-form prose, there is no per-run structured audit record of the seven fields the remit requires, and Loki is gated behind a LOKI_URL hardcoded to None.
